lesson.menu_desc: First and second conditionals + objection vocabulary -- 29 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 12 -- Objections: interrupting with authority in court.
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Luiz Bressane | Aula 12 -- Objections

listenings.text: Before we begin, let me explain to the new attorneys how objections work in this court. An objection is your tool to protect the record, but timing is everything. If a question calls for speculation, you must object before the witness answers. If you wait, the harm is done, and I can only strike the answer from the record. When you object, give me one ground: relevance, foundation, hearsay, or that the question is argumentative. If the objection is valid, I will sustain it; if it is not, I will overrule it and the witness will answer. If the matter is sensitive, ask for a sidebar and we will discuss it away from the jury. Above all, do not interject while a colleague is speaking. Rise, say objection, and wait for me. If you would interrupt with authority, you must first interrupt with respect.

You are listening to the legal affairs review. In court today, the defense raised a series of well-timed objections that shaped the trial. When the prosecutor asked a question with no foundation, counsel rose at once and the judge sustained the objection. Later, an answer that was clearly prejudicial was struck from the record. If the defense had stayed silent, that statement would have reached the jury. At one point the lawyers requested a sidebar, and after a brief discussion the disputed evidence was ruled inadmissible. Reporters were told that if the trial continues at this pace, a verdict could come by Friday. We will follow the case as it develops.
